commonFlags.description: Single login (`-l admin`) or list of logins (`-L users.txt`).
Single password or wordlist of passwords.
Parallel tasks (per host). Default 16; lower it (1-4) for fragile services.
Stop after the first valid pair on the current host.
Stop globally — across all hosts in -M list — on first hit.
Verbose: print every attempt; -vV adds module-internal detail.
Write found credentials to FILE (use -b to choose JSON / JSONv1 format).
Override the default service port (e.g. SSH on 2222).
Try empty password (n), login==password (s), reversed login (r).
List of targets (one host per line) for sweeping a subnet.
Brute-force generator instead of a wordlist (a=letters, 1=digits, A=upper, !=symbols).
Wait between connections — slows things down to dodge rate limits.

modules.name: ssh
modules.summary: OpenSSH password auth. The classic Hydra target.

modules.name: http-get / http-head
modules.summary: HTTP Basic / Digest auth via GET or HEAD.

modules.name: http-post-form
modules.summary: HTML form login. The trickiest module — needs a colon-delimited spec.

modules.name: smb / smb2
modules.summary: SMB authentication (NTLM). Trips Windows account lockouts fast.

modules.name: rdp
modules.summary: Remote Desktop Protocol. Slow, noisy, and triggers lockouts immediately.

modules.name: ldap2 / ldap3
modules.summary: LDAP simple bind (v2 or v3). Use ldap3 unless the server is ancient.

modules.name: imap / pop3 / smtp
modules.summary: Mail-protocol auth, with TLS variants (imaps, pop3s, smtps).

formAttackParts.label: Path
formAttackParts.explain: URL path that handles the form POST.

formAttackParts.label: Body template
formAttackParts.explain: POST body. ^USER^ and ^PASS^ are substituted from the wordlists.

formAttackParts.label: Failure marker
formAttackParts.explain: String that appears on a failed login. Hydra treats anything else as success.

formAttackParts.label: Success marker
formAttackParts.explain: Alternative — string that appears on success. Use F or S, not both.

formAttackParts.label: Cookie (optional)
formAttackParts.explain: Pre-fetch this URL to seed cookies, then attack with them.

recipes.title: SSH brute force with stop-on-success
recipes.details: Single user, password list, low concurrency to avoid OpenSSH MaxStartups throttling. -f stops the moment one works.

recipes.title: User+password lists, stop globally
recipes.details: Cartesian product across the subnet. -F kills everything when the first credential lands. CIDR is supported in URL form.

recipes.title: Combo file from a leak
recipes.details: Each line in creds.txt is `user:pass`. No cartesian explosion — just walks the pairs as given.

recipes.title: HTTP form login
recipes.details: Most authenticated web apps. Capture the exact form body in Burp first; the failure string must NOT appear on a successful login.

recipes.title: RDP password spray
recipes.details: Single password against many users with throttling — far less likely to lock accounts than -P. -W spaces out attempts.

recipes.title: Empty / same-as-username / reversed
recipes.details: Three free guesses per user before any wordlist is touched. Catches lazy defaults like `admin:admin`.

recipes.title: Brute-force generator
recipes.details: No wordlist — just generate every 4-6 char alphanumeric password. Use sparingly; the search space explodes with length.

tips: Capture the real login request in Burp first. Hydra's form-string assumes the body matches exactly — extra hidden fields or CSRF tokens will silently break the attack.
Lower -t (to 1-4) on services with rate limits. SSH, RDP, SMB will start dropping or banning at default concurrency.
Use -e nsr before any wordlist run — three free shots per user that catch most lazy defaults.
Verify the failure marker matches a failed login but NOT a successful one. The most common Hydra bug is a marker that hits both, returning all-true or all-false.
For password spraying (1 password × many users), set -t 1 with -W to space attempts and avoid lockouts.
-o writes hits to disk — always use it; otherwise you'll lose results when the terminal scrolls.
Hydra is loud. On real engagements, decide first whether brute force is acceptable scope; password spraying is usually safer than account-by-account brute force.
limitations: Online brute force is loud and trips logging / lockouts on most production services. It's a CTF / lab tool first; spraying is the only sane production variant.
Form attacks are fragile — any change to the login flow (CSRF tokens, JS-set values, redirects) breaks the spec until you re-capture it.
Rate-limited or CAPTCHA-protected endpoints defeat Hydra entirely. No amount of -t tuning helps.
MFA / 2FA stops Hydra dead — knowing the password isn't enough. Test environments without MFA only.
Cracking credentials on services you do not own without written authorisation is illegal in most jurisdictions.
